curriculum.title: Python for Advanced Learners
curriculum.description: Master advanced Python concepts including decorators, generators, metaprogramming, asynchronous programming, and design patterns. Build sophisticated applications with professional-grade code.

lessons.title: Advanced Decorators
lessons.body: Create powerful decorators with arguments, nested decorators, and decorator classes.
lessons.avatar_script: Decorators are functions that enhance other functions!

questions.question: What is a decorator with arguments?
options: A function that modifies another function
A decorator factory that returns decorators
questions.answer: A decorator factory that returns decorators
questions.explanation: Decorators with arguments use a factory pattern with three levels of nesting.

questions.question: *args and **kwargs allow functions to accept any number of arguments.
questions.explanation: *args captures positional arguments as a tuple, **kwargs captures keyword arguments as a dictionary.

questions.question: Create a repeat decorator
questions.explanation: Create a decorator factory named repeat(times) that makes a function run multiple times.

code_example.explanation: Start by typing def repeat(times): followed by a colon to create the outer function that takes the number of repetitions as a parameter. Press Enter and indent. Then define the actual decorator function by typing def decorator(func): and add a colon. Press Enter and indent again. Inside this decorator, create a wrapper function by typing def wrapper(*args, **kwargs): and add a colon. Press Enter and indent. Within the wrapper, use a for loop to repeat the function execution: for i in range(times): followed by a colon. Press Enter, indent, and type func(*args, **kwargs) to call the decorated function. After the loop, type return wrapper on a new line at the decorator's indentation level. Finally, return the decorator from the outer function on another new line at the repeat function's indentation level by typing return decorator.

lessons.title: Generators and Iterators
lessons.body: Create efficient iterators using generators with yield statements.
lessons.avatar_script: Generate values lazily for memory efficiency!

questions.question: What does yield do in a generator?
options: Returns a value and pauses execution
questions.answer: Returns a value and pauses execution
questions.explanation: yield returns a value and suspends the function, resuming from where it left off on the next call.

questions.question: Generators are more memory efficient than lists.
questions.explanation: Generators produce values on-the-fly instead of storing all values in memory at once.

questions.question: Create a Fibonacci generator
questions.explanation: Write a generator function fibonacci(n) that yields Fibonacci numbers until it reaches n.

code_example.explanation: Start by typing def fibonacci(n): followed by a colon to define a generator function. Initialize a and b to 0 and 1. Use a while loop (while a < n:) and yield a to produce each value. Then update with a, b = b, a + b to move to the next Fibonacci number.

lessons.title: Lambda and Functional Techniques
lessons.body: Use lambda functions with map, filter, reduce, and compose functions.

code_example.code:
from functools import reduce

numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
product = reduce(lambda x, y: x * y, numbers)
print(product)

questions.question: What does reduce do?
options: Combines all items into a single value
questions.answer: Combines all items into a single value
questions.explanation: reduce applies a function cumulatively to items, reducing them to a single value.

questions.question: reduce is available directly in the Python built-ins.
questions.explanation: reduce was moved to functools module in Python 3.

questions.question: Calculate factorial using reduce
questions.explanation: Calculate the factorial of 5 using reduce to multiply numbers 1 to 5.

code_example.code:
from functools import reduce
factorial = reduce(lambda x, y: x * y, range(1, 6))
code_example.explanation: Start by importing reduce from the functools module. Then call reduce with a lambda that multiplies two values, across range(1, 6). This multiplies 1×2×3×4×5 to produce 120 (5!).

lessons.title: Metaclasses and Type System
lessons.body: Understand metaclasses and how to customize class creation and behavior.

questions.question: What is a metaclass?
options: A class whose instances are classes
questions.answer: A class whose instances are classes
questions.explanation: Metaclasses are classes that create other classes, allowing customization of class creation.

questions.question: All classes have a metaclass.
questions.explanation: Every class in Python is an instance of a metaclass, by default the type metaclass.

questions.question: Create a logging metaclass
questions.explanation: Create a metaclass that prints a message when a class is created.

code_example.explanation: Start by defining class LoggingMeta(type). Inside it, override __new__(mcs, name, bases, dct) to print the class name, then call super().__new__(mcs, name, bases, dct) to create and return the new class.

lessons.title: Properties and Descriptors
lessons.body: Control attribute access using properties and descriptor protocol.
lessons.avatar_script: Fine-tune data access in your classes!

questions.question: What does @property decorator do?
options: Makes a method accessible like an attribute
questions.answer: Makes a method accessible like an attribute
questions.explanation: @property allows getter methods to be called like attribute access without parentheses.

questions.question: Properties can have both getters and setters.
questions.explanation: You can define getter with @property and setter with @attribute_name.setter.

questions.question: Create age property with validation
questions.explanation: Create a Person class where age is a property that validates values before setting them.

code_example.explanation: Start by defining class Person and storing the internal value on self._age. Then add an @property getter that returns self._age and an @age.setter that checks the incoming value and raises ValueError if it's out of range before assigning.

lessons.title: Context Managers
lessons.body: Create custom context managers using context manager protocol.

questions.question: What does __enter__ do in a context manager?
options: Enters the with block and returns a resource
questions.answer: Enters the with block and returns a resource
questions.explanation: __enter__ is called when entering a with block and typically returns a resource to use.

questions.question: __exit__ is guaranteed to run even if exceptions occur.
questions.explanation: __exit__ always runs when exiting the with block, ensuring cleanup happens.

questions.question: Create a database connection context manager
questions.explanation: Create a context manager class that sets up a connection in __enter__ and cleans up in __exit__.

code_example.explanation: Start by typing class DatabaseConnection: then define __init__ to store db_name. Implement __enter__ to print a connect message, set a connection value, and return it. Implement __exit__ to print a disconnect message and reset the connection.

lessons.title: Async and Await Basics
lessons.body: Write asynchronous code using async/await syntax for concurrent operations.

code_example.code:
import asyncio

async def fetch_data():
    await asyncio.sleep(1)
    return 'Data fetched'

async def main():
    result = await fetch_data()
    print(result)

asyncio.run(main())

questions.question: What does await do?
options: Pauses execution until a coroutine completes
questions.answer: Pauses execution until a coroutine completes
questions.explanation: await suspends the coroutine until the awaited coroutine completes.

questions.question: asyncio.run() executes an async function.
questions.explanation: asyncio.run() is the entry point to run an async function from synchronous code.

questions.question: Create async function that downloads files
questions.explanation: Write async code that downloads multiple files concurrently and prints the results.

code_example.code:
async def download_file(filename):
    await asyncio.sleep(2)
    return f'{filename} downloaded'

async def main():
    tasks = [download_file('file1.txt'), download_file('file2.txt'), download_file('file3.txt')]
    results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
    print(results)

asyncio.run(main())
code_example.explanation: Start by defining async def download_file(filename) that awaits asyncio.sleep and returns a message. In main(), create a list of download coroutines and await asyncio.gather(*tasks) to run them concurrently. Finally, call asyncio.run(main()).

lessons.title: Coroutines and Tasks
lessons.body: Master coroutines, tasks, and concurrent execution patterns.

code_example.code:
import asyncio

async def task1():
    for i in range(3):
        print(f'Task 1: {i}')
        await asyncio.sleep(1)

async def task2():
    for i in range(3):
        print(f'Task 2: {i}')
        await asyncio.sleep(1)

async def main():
    await asyncio.gather(task1(), task2())

questions.question: What is a coroutine?
options: A function that runs in parallel
A function that can pause and resume
questions.answer: A function that can pause and resume
questions.explanation: Coroutines are functions defined with async that can pause at await points.

questions.question: asyncio.gather() runs tasks concurrently.
questions.explanation: gather() schedules multiple coroutines to run concurrently and waits for all to complete.

questions.question: Create producer-consumer pattern
questions.explanation: Create a producer and consumer that communicate using an asyncio.Queue.

code_example.code:
async def producer(queue):
    for i in range(5):
        await queue.put(i)
        await asyncio.sleep(1)
    await queue.put(None)

async def consumer(queue):
    while True:
        item = await queue.get()
        if item is None:
            break
        print(f'Consumed: {item}')

async def main():
    queue = asyncio.Queue()
    await asyncio.gather(producer(queue), consumer(queue))
code_example.explanation: Start by defining a producer(queue) that puts items into the queue and uses None as a stop signal. Define a consumer(queue) that reads items in a loop and stops when it sees None. In main(), create asyncio.Queue() and run both with asyncio.gather.

lessons.title: Error Handling in Async Code
lessons.body: Handle exceptions and errors in asynchronous programs effectively.

code_example.code:
async def risky_operation():
    raise ValueError('Something went wrong')

async def main():
    try:
        result = await risky_operation()
    except ValueError as e:
        print(f'Caught error: {e}')

questions.question: How do you handle exceptions in async functions?
options: Exceptions cannot be caught in async
Use a special async try block
questions.explanation: Exception handling in async works the same as sync code using try-except.

questions.question: asyncio.gather() propagates exceptions from tasks.
questions.explanation: If any task in gather() raises an exception, it will be raised when gather() completes.

questions.question: Handle multiple async errors with gather
questions.explanation: Use asyncio.gather with try/except to handle errors from concurrent async tasks.

code_example.code:
async def fetch(url):
    if 'error' in url:
        raise ConnectionError(f'Failed to fetch {url}')
    await asyncio.sleep(1)
    return f'Fetched {url}'

async def main():
    urls = ['url1', 'url2', 'error_url', 'url3']
    try:
        tasks = [fetch(url) for url in urls]
        results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
    except ConnectionError as e:
        print(f'Error: {e}')
code_example.explanation: Start by creating async functions that might fail. Type async def fetch(url): followed by a colon and press Enter and indent. Add error handling with if 'error' in url: followed by a colon, then press Enter and indent to type raise ConnectionError(f'Failed to fetch {url}'). Otherwise, type await asyncio.sleep(1) and then return f'Fetched {url}'. Create a main function by typing async def main(): followed by a colon. Press Enter and indent. Create a list of URLs with urls = ['url1', 'url2', 'error_url', 'url3']. Then wrap gathering tasks in try-except: type try: followed by a colon and press Enter and indent. Type tasks = [fetch(url) for url in urls] to create a list comprehension of coroutines. Then type results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks) to run all concurrently. Unindent and type except ConnectionError as e: followed by a colon and indent. Type print(f'Error: {e}') to handle any connection errors.

lessons.title: Building a High-Performance Application
lessons.body: Combine advanced concepts to create a sophisticated real-world application.

questions.question: What does @abstractmethod do?
options: Forces subclasses to implement a method
questions.answer: Forces subclasses to implement a method
questions.explanation: @abstractmethod defines an interface that all subclasses must implement.

questions.question: You can instantiate an abstract base class.
questions.explanation: Abstract base classes cannot be instantiated directly; only their concrete subclasses can be.

questions.question: Create abstract storage strategy
questions.explanation: Create an abstract storage strategy interface and concrete classes that implement save().

code_example.explanation: Start by importing the necessary modules: from abc import ABC, abstractmethod. Then type class StorageStrategy(ABC): followed by a colon and add an @abstractmethod save(self, data). After that, create concrete classes like FileStorage and DatabaseStorage that implement save in different ways.
